name: SupabaseSync
tagline: The CloudSync offline-first app with a REAL cloud database: Supabase (hosted Postgres). Same Room-backed architecture; only the CloudApi implementation changed.
whatItDoes: The same single-screen notes app as CloudSync — add/edit/delete with per-row sync badges and an online/offline + pending banner, all driven by the local Room database — but the real cloud is now Supabase, i.e. hosted Postgres reached through its auto-generated REST API. Push is an upsert into a 'notes' table; pull is a filtered, ordered select on the updated_at column. By default it runs offline against the in-memory FakeCloudApi, so it builds, runs, and unit-tests with no Supabase project; create the table, add your project URL + anon key to local.properties (git-ignored), and the app syncs against real Postgres automatically — no source edit, no flag to flip.
mentalModel: Keep CloudSync's mental model exactly — the UI reads only local Room, a background worker reconciles with the cloud — and notice that swapping the cloud is a ONE-CLASS change. Here the cloud is a Postgres TABLE, so the two operations map straight onto SQL: pull is SELECT ... WHERE updated_at > cursor, push is INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (an upsert).

screens.name: SupabaseSyncApp
screens.role: The whole screen (identical to CloudSync): add/edit form, Sync now + Simulate remote edit, the online/offline + pending banner, and the notes list. Reads Room-backed StateFlows only.

screens.name: SyncStatusBar
screens.role: Green (Online) / amber (Offline — changes queued) banner with the not-yet-synced count.

screens.name: NoteCard
screens.role: One note row with a colour-coded sync badge; tap to edit, button to delete.

screens.name: SyncBadge
screens.role: Per-row ✓ Synced / ⏳ Pending / ⚠ Failed, from the row's SyncState.

concepts.plain: Supabase wraps a real Postgres database with an auto-generated REST API (PostgREST). supabase-kt turns that into typed Kotlin calls against a table — here a 'notes' table whose columns mirror NoteDto.

push(note) does from("notes").upsert(note) { select() }.decodeSingle(). upsert is INSERT, or UPDATE if a row with this primary key already exists; the trailing select() asks Postgres to RETURN the stored row, which we decode as the server copy.

pullSince(since) does from("notes").select { filter { gt("updated_at", since) }; order("updated_at", ASCENDING) }.decodeList() — only rows changed after the cursor, oldest first, decoded straight into NoteDto.

Postgres columns are conventionally snake_case but Kotlin properties are camelCase, so NoteDto maps updatedAt to the column with @SerialName("updated_at"). The other names (id/title/body/deleted) match directly. supabase-kt uses kotlinx.serialization, so NoteDto stays @Serializable.

createSupabaseClient(url, anonKey) { install(Postgrest) } builds one client; the url + anonKey come from BuildConfig.SUPABASE_URL / SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, which build.gradle.kts feeds in from local.properties (git-ignored). supabase-kt sends HTTP through the Ktor engine (ktor-client-okhttp) on the classpath. No local cache — that is exactly why we keep Room as the offline source of truth.

Postgres enforces access with RLS policies. For the demo you enable RLS on 'notes' and add open policies so the public anon key can read/write; in production you would scope those policies to authenticated users.

Keys never live in committed source: build.gradle.kts reads supabase.url / supabase.anonKey from local.properties (git-ignored) into BuildConfig.SUPABASE_URL / SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, and provideCloudApi auto-uses real Supabase when both are present, the offline Fake when blank — so there is no flag to flip and nothing to paste into CloudApi.kt.

Compared to CloudSync, only CloudApi.kt's real implementation, NoteDto's @SerialName, and the build dependencies changed. The entity, DAO, repository, SyncWorker, SyncScheduler, ViewModel, UI, and unit tests are identical — the payoff of hiding the backend behind an interface.

files.role: The CloudApi interface + SupabaseCloudApi (the REAL cloud, reading BuildConfig) + the offline FakeCloudApi + provideCloudApi (auto-selects real vs Fake).
Optimistic writes, sync() push→pull, and the pure shouldAcceptRemote last-write-wins rule (unchanged from CloudSync).
Enqueues the SyncWorker with a NetworkType.CONNECTED constraint + backoff (unchanged).
Reads local.properties → BuildConfig.SUPABASE_* (buildConfig = true); adds the supabase-kt BoM + postgrest-kt + a Ktor engine + kotlinx-serialization-json; drops Retrofit/OkHttp.

highlights.title: Sync bookkeeping: the entity + a column-mapped DTO
highlights.explain: The @Entity Note is unchanged from CloudSync (client id, updatedAt clock, syncState, deleted tombstone). NoteDto stays @Serializable (supabase-kt speaks JSON) but adds @SerialName("updated_at") so the camelCase Kotlin property maps to the snake_case Postgres column — the single place that naming boundary is bridged.

highlights.title: The same tiny cloud interface CloudSync used
highlights.explain: Identical to CloudSync: pullSince(cursor) + push(note). Everything above the data layer depends only on this, which is why moving from a Retrofit stub to real Supabase changes nothing else in the app.

highlights.title: The REAL cloud — Supabase (Postgres)
highlights.explain: This is the whole 'cloud is now defined' change. createSupabaseClient(url, anonKey) { install(Postgrest) } builds the client (url/key from BuildConfig, fed from local.properties). pullSince() is from("notes").select { filter { gt("updated_at", since) }; order(...) } decoded to a List<NoteDto>; push() is from("notes").upsert(note) { select() } decoded to one NoteDto. Those map directly onto SELECT ... WHERE and INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE. The repository, worker, and UI never see any of this.

highlights.title: Optimistic local writes (Room first, marked PENDING)
highlights.explain: Unchanged from CloudSync: addNote writes to Room immediately with a fresh UUID and PENDING — no network here. The Supabase upsert happens later in the background worker, so a new note shows instantly whether or not Supabase is reachable.

highlights.title: sync(): push the outbox, then pull remote changes
highlights.explain: Identical engine: upsert every PENDING row (FAILED + rethrow on error so the worker retries), then select rows newer than MAX(updatedAt) and merge each through shouldAcceptRemote. The only thing that changed since CloudSync is what cloud.push / cloud.pullSince do under the hood.

highlights.title: Last-write-wins, as a pure function
highlights.explain: The same pure conflict rule, unit-tested with no Supabase: accept a remote row only if we have no local copy or ours is SYNCED and not newer; a still-PENDING local edit wins until it is pushed.

highlights.title: Why offline 'just queues': the network constraint
highlights.explain: Unchanged: the NetworkType.CONNECTED constraint defers the sync until there is a connection, APPEND_OR_REPLACE keeps a write made mid-sync, and backoff handles transient Supabase/network errors. Offline-first behaviour is identical regardless of which real backend is plugged in.

newVsPrev: FirebaseSync wired a real cloud as a document store (Firestore). SupabaseSync plugs the SAME CloudApi seam into a relational one: Supabase = hosted Postgres via supabase-kt, where push is an upsert and pull is a filtered select, and NoteDto gains @SerialName("updated_at") to bridge camelCase to the snake_case column. The contrast is the lesson: Firestore bundles its own offline cache, Supabase brings none — which is exactly why both lean on Room as the local source of truth. Same architecture, two real backends, two SDK idioms.
tryThis: Run it as-is (offline): identical to CloudSync/FirebaseSync — notes go Pending then Synced against the in-memory fake, with no Supabase setup.

Create a Supabase project, run the create-table SQL from the README (with the demo RLS policies), and add supabase.url + supabase.anonKey to local.properties (git-ignored). Rebuild and run on a networked device — provideCloudApi auto-detects the keys (no source edit, no flag).
With real Supabase, add a note and watch the row appear in the Supabase Table Editor (id == the note's UUID, updated_at == the millis clock).
Insert a row directly in the Table Editor with a larger updated_at and watch the app PULL it in on the next sync.
Open Note.kt and change a column name in the SQL without updating the matching @SerialName — observe the decode break, proving where the Kotlin↔Postgres name boundary lives.
gotchas: Runs OFFLINE until you create the table and add your URL + anon key to local.properties (supabase.url / supabase.anonKey); then build.gradle.kts feeds them into BuildConfig and provideCloudApi auto-detects them. There is no flag to flip and nothing to paste into source.
You must create the 'notes' table first (SQL in the README). Column names are snake_case (updated_at) — that is why NoteDto uses @SerialName("updated_at").

Access is governed by Row Level Security. The README's demo policies are wide open so the anon key works; tighten them before storing anything real (the anon key is publishable, but only as safe as your policies).
supabase-kt is pinned to 3.2.2 (built against Kotlin 2.2, matching this repo). Newer 3.x releases target Kotlin 2.3 and can clash with the 2.2.10 compiler — bump deliberately.
supabase-kt needs a Ktor engine on the classpath (ktor-client-okhttp here); without one the client cannot make HTTP calls.
runTest: Offline by default, so it builds and unit-tests with no Supabase project: ./gradlew :compileDebugKotlin, then ./gradlew :testDebugUnitTest (the last-write-wins rule, the SyncState converter, and the fake-cloud round trip — none need a network), then ./gradlew installDebug. To go live, create the notes table (README SQL), add your URL + anon key to local.properties (supabase.url / supabase.anonKey), and run on a networked device — the app auto-detects them via BuildConfig.
